title: How Transformers Learn to Speak
subtitle: From raw text to fluent generation: tokens, training, and the dials behind every answer
theme: Trace the full life of language inside a model: how text becomes numbers, how the model learns, and how it generates.
learningObjectives: Explain tokenization (BPE/WordPiece) and why models work in subword pieces rather than whole words or letters
Describe embeddings and how tokens become meaningful vectors
Understand the training objective (next-token and masked) and how loss + softmax drive learning
Explain teacher forcing and causal attention masks during training
Define what a 'context window' really is and why it matters in 2026
Compare decoding strategies — greedy, beam, temperature, top-k, top-p — and predict their effect on output
storyHook: We've built the engine and watched a sentence flow through it. But a fresh Transformer is a newborn with a perfect brain and absolutely nothing to say. Today is the story of how it learns to speak — how raw text gets chopped into digestible pieces, turned into numbers full of meaning, and how, through a single relentless game played billions of times, a blank model becomes something that can write poetry, code, and arguments. Then we'll reach for the dials that decide whether it sounds like a cautious accountant or a wild poet.

slides.title: The Newborn Model: From Silence to Speech
slides.body:
## A perfect brain with nothing to say

After Day 2, you can describe the Transformer's architecture. But an untrained Transformer is **useless** — its weights are random, and if you ask it anything it produces gibberish. Today is the story of how that newborn learns language.

The journey has three acts:

### Act 1 — Text becomes numbers
Models can't read letters; they do math on vectors. So we need:
- **Tokenization** — chopping text into pieces the model can handle.
- **Embeddings** — turning each piece into a meaningful vector.

### Act 2 — The model learns
- **The training objective** — the single game (predict the next token) played billions of times.
- **Loss & softmax** — how we measure 'how wrong' and push the model to improve.
- **Teacher forcing & masks** — the clever tricks that make training fast and honest.

### Act 3 — The model speaks
- **Decoding strategies** — greedy, beam, temperature, top-k, top-p — the dials that shape every answer you've ever gotten from an AI.

```
RAW TEXT → [tokenize] → [embed] → [Transformer] → [predict next] → DECODE → TEXT
           Act 1                    Act 2                       Act 3
```

> By the end of today, you'll understand the *complete* life of a sentence — from typing it in, to the model learning from billions like it, to the moment it generates a reply.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame the day as a coming-of-age story: the model is a newborn with a perfect brain and nothing to say. Make clear that architecture (Day 2) is necessary but not sufficient — without training it's gibberish. Preview the three acts and promise the 'dials' payoff at the end (why the same model can be boring or creative). Ask: 'A baby has a fully-formed brain at birth but can't speak — what does it need? Exposure to language, over and over.' That's pretraining.
slides.marketConnection: The 'newborn brain' is the base model; turning it into a helpful assistant adds later stages (instruction tuning, RLHF) — a distinction every 2026 buyer should know when comparing a raw open-weights model to a polished commercial chatbot.

slides.title: Tokenization: Why Models Speak in Puzzle Pieces
slides.body:
## The first decision: what is a 'unit' of text?

Before a model can process text, it must chop it into units called **tokens**. There are three obvious options, and the winner is a clever compromise.

### Option A — whole words
'I love pizza' → ['I', 'love', 'pizza']. *Problem:* the vocabulary becomes gigantic (millions of words), and any new or misspelled word ('pizzaaa', 'antidisestablishmentarianism') is an unknown the model has never seen.

### Option B — individual characters
'pizza' → ['p','i','z','z','a']. *Problem:* sequences become extremely long and the model has to relearn that 'p-i-z-z-a' means pizza every time. Inefficient.

### Option C (the winner) — subword pieces
Break words into frequent chunks. 'unbelievable' → ['un', 'believ', 'able']. Common words stay whole; rare words break into reusable parts.

```
"tokenization"  → ["token", "ization"]
"unhappiness"   → ["un", "happiness"]
"ChatGPT"       → ["Chat", "G", "PT"]
"😀"            → may be multiple byte-tokens
```

### How the pieces are chosen: BPE & WordPiece

**Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** (used by GPT) starts from characters and repeatedly **merges the most frequent adjacent pair** into a new token, over and over, until it has the desired vocabulary size (often ~30k-100k+ tokens). Frequent sequences ('ing', 'tion') become single tokens; rare ones stay split.

**WordPiece** (used by BERT) is similar but chooses merges that most improve the model's likelihood of the data rather than pure frequency.

### Why this is brilliant
- **No unknown words** — anything can be built from smaller pieces, down to bytes.
- **Manageable vocabulary** — tens of thousands, not millions.
- **Efficient** — common words are one token; rare words cost a few.

> A model never sees 'words.' It sees a stream of subword puzzle pieces. This is why models sometimes miscount letters or fumble wordplay — they don't see letters, they see chunks.
slides.speakerNotes: Walk the three options and let the class feel why whole-words and characters both fail — then subwords as the 'Goldilocks' answer. Live demo idea: ask 'how would you tokenize unbelievable?' and let them propose pieces. The killer insight to leave them with: models see CHUNKS, not letters — this is the real reason older models struggled with 'how many r's in strawberry.' Ask: 'If the model never sees individual letters, why might it miscount them?' This explains a famous failure mode.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, you literally pay per token — API pricing, context limits, and speed are all measured in tokens. Knowing that 1 token is roughly 4 characters / 0.75 words in English lets you estimate cost and whether your document fits in a context window.

slides.title: Embeddings: Giving Tokens Meaning
slides.body:
## Turning puzzle pieces into vectors of meaning

Once text is tokens, each token is just an ID number (e.g., 'pizza' = 15043). Numbers-as-IDs carry no meaning — ID 15043 isn't 'more than' ID 15042 in any useful way. So we replace each token ID with a learned vector called an **embedding**.

An embedding is a list of numbers (say, 768 or 4096 of them) that represents the token's *meaning* as a point in a high-dimensional space.

### The magic: meaning becomes geometry

During training, the model arranges these vectors so that **similar meanings sit close together** and relationships become directions:

```
king  - man  + woman  ≈ queen
Paris - France + Italy ≈ Rome
```

These famous analogies work because the model learned to encode concepts (royalty, gender, 'capital-of') as consistent *directions* in the space. Words used in similar contexts end up as neighbors.

### The 'map of meaning' analogy

Imagine a vast map where every concept has a location. 'Dog' and 'puppy' are neighbors; 'dog' and 'democracy' are far apart. 'Hot' and 'cold' sit on opposite ends of a temperature axis. The embedding space is exactly such a map, but with thousands of dimensions instead of two.

### Two crucial points

1. **Embeddings are learned, not hand-coded.** They start random and become meaningful purely from training on text.
2. **Context refines them.** The embedding is the *starting* meaning; then (Day 2) self-attention adjusts each token's vector based on its neighbors — so 'bank' near 'river' ends up different from 'bank' near 'money.' The embedding is the seed; attention grows it into context.

> Tokenization gives the model pieces. Embeddings give those pieces *meaning* — turning language into geometry the math can work on.
slides.speakerNotes: The 'king - man + woman = queen' example is iconic — write it on the board, it always gets a reaction. Drive the core metaphor: embeddings = a giant MAP of meaning where distance = similarity and directions = relationships. Crucial nuance to land: the embedding is just the STARTING meaning; attention then contextualizes it (bank/river vs bank/money). Ask: 'How would you place hot, warm, cool, cold on a map so the geometry captures their relationship?' (A line — that's a learned axis.)
slides.marketConnection: Embeddings are a product category of their own in 2026: 'embedding models' power semantic search, recommendation, and the retrieval step of RAG systems. When an enterprise 'lets you chat with your documents,' it's embedding those docs into this map and finding the nearest neighbors to your question.

slides.title: The Training Game: Predict the Next Token, Forever
slides.body:
## One simple game, played a trillion times

Here is the astonishing secret of how models like GPT learn to do *everything* — write code, reason, translate, console you: they play **one** game over and over.

### The next-token prediction game

Take a sentence from the training data. Show the model all the words up to some point. Ask it: **'What word comes next?'** Then reveal the true next word and nudge the model to have predicted it better. Repeat across the entire internet's worth of text.

```
Input:  "The capital of France is"
Model guesses a probability for EVERY token in its vocabulary:
   "Paris" → 0.61
   "London" → 0.08
   "a" → 0.05
   ... (50,000 more)
True answer: "Paris"  → push the model to raise P("Paris")
```

### Why this trivial game produces intelligence

To predict the next word *really well* across billions of diverse texts, the model is forced to *implicitly learn*:
- **Grammar** (to predict 'is' vs 'are')
- **Facts** (to predict 'Paris' after 'capital of France is')
- **Reasoning** (to complete 'If all men are mortal and Socrates is a man, then Socrates is...')
- **Style, code syntax, even arithmetic patterns**

Nobody told the model these things explicitly. They are all just *compressed strategies for the next-token game.* This is the profound idea behind **self-supervised learning**: the text itself provides the answers (the next word), so we need no human labels — just oceans of raw text.

### The masked variant (BERT-style)

Decoder models (GPT) predict the *next* token. Encoder models (BERT) play a slightly different game: **masked language modeling** — blank out random words in the middle and predict them using *both* left and right context. ('The [MASK] sat on the mat' → 'cat'.) Same spirit, bidirectional flavor — great for understanding rather than generation.

> The whole towering edifice of modern AI rests on this one humble game: guess the next piece, get corrected, repeat — at planetary scale.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the most mind-blowing idea of the whole course — deliver it with awe. The punchline: a SINGLE trivial game (predict the next token), played at planetary scale, forces the model to learn grammar, facts, and reasoning as side effects. Stress 'self-supervised = no human labels needed, the text grades itself.' Contrast next-token (GPT, generation) vs masked (BERT, understanding). Ask: 'To reliably finish the sentence about Socrates, what does the model secretly have to have learned?' (Logic.) Let that land.
slides.marketConnection: The 'just predict the next token at scale' recipe drove the 2020-2024 scaling boom. By 2026 the frontier added new training stages on top (reasoning via reinforcement learning, where models are rewarded for correct step-by-step solutions) — but next-token pretraining is still the foundation every model is built on.

slides.title: Softmax & Loss: Measuring and Punishing Wrongness
slides.body:
## How the model knows it was wrong — and by how much

The training game needs two machines: one that turns the model's raw outputs into probabilities (**softmax**), and one that measures how wrong those probabilities were (**loss**).

### Softmax: raw scores → probabilities

The model's final layer outputs a raw score (a 'logit') for every token in the vocabulary. **Softmax** converts those into a clean probability distribution — all positive, all summing to 1:

```
logits:    Paris=3.2   London=1.1   pizza=-0.5
softmax →  Paris=0.78   London=0.10  pizza=0.04 ...  (sums to 1.0)
```

Softmax exaggerates differences: bigger logits get disproportionately bigger probabilities. (We met softmax inside attention on Day 2 — same function, different job: there it weighted Values; here it picks next tokens.)

### Cross-entropy loss: the wrongness meter

Now we compare the model's probability for the *correct* token to the ideal (which is 1.0). The standard measure is **cross-entropy loss**:

```
loss = -log( probability the model gave to the correct token )
```

- Model said correct token had P = 0.78 → loss = -log(0.78) ≈ 0.25 (small — good!)
- Model said correct token had P = 0.02 → loss = -log(0.02) ≈ 3.9 (large — bad!)

The more confident the model was in the *right* answer, the lower the loss. The more confident it was in a *wrong* answer, the more it gets punished. The logarithm makes the punishment for confident-wrongness brutal — which teaches the model humility.

### Backpropagation: turning loss into learning

The loss is a single number: 'how wrong were you?' The training algorithm then computes how to nudge **every weight** in the model to reduce that number — this is **backpropagation + gradient descent**. Do this on trillions of examples and the random newborn slowly becomes fluent.

```
guess → softmax → compare to truth → loss → backprop → tweak weights → repeat
```

> Softmax says 'here's my bet.' Cross-entropy says 'here's how badly you bet.' Backprop says 'here's how to bet better next time.'
slides.speakerNotes: Keep this concrete with the numbers. Two key intuitions: (1) softmax = turn raw scores into a confident bet that sums to 100%; (2) cross-entropy with the -log = confident-wrong is punished savagely, which is exactly what we want. Use the two worked loss values (0.25 vs 3.9) to show the asymmetry. Don't go deep into backprop math — just convey 'loss is one number measuring wrongness, and the optimizer crawls back through the network to reduce it.' Ask: 'Why punish a confident wrong answer more than an unsure one?' (Confidence in falsehood is the worst failure.)
slides.marketConnection: When you see training charts in 2026 model releases ('loss curves'), this cross-entropy loss dropping over billions of tokens is exactly what's being plotted — a falling curve is the model literally getting less wrong over time.

slides.title: Teacher Forcing & Attention Masks: Training Honestly and Fast
slides.body:
## Two clever tricks that make training work

Training a generator has a chicken-and-egg problem and a cheating problem. Two mechanisms solve them.

### Problem 1: How do you train a model that generates step-by-step?
If the model generates word 1 wrong, word 2 is built on a mistake, and the whole sequence derails — training would be chaos.

**Solution: Teacher forcing.** During training, instead of feeding the model its *own* (possibly wrong) previous guess, we feed it the **correct** previous word from the training data. It's like a piano teacher placing your fingers on the right keys so you learn the right pattern, even if you'd have fumbled.

```
Target: "Je suis heureux"
step1: input <start>      → model predicts next, we grade it, but...
step2: input <start> Je   → we feed the TRUE "Je", not the model's guess
step3: input <start> Je suis → feed TRUE "Je suis"
```

This lets every position be trained **in parallel** (huge speedup) and keeps training stable. (Side note: it creates a small train/generate mismatch called 'exposure bias' — at inference the model must rely on its own outputs — but the benefits dominate.)

### Problem 2: How do we stop the model from peeking at the answer?
We feed the whole correct sentence at once for parallelism — but when predicting word 3, the model must NOT be allowed to see words 4, 5, 6 (that would be cheating; it'd just copy the answer).

**Solution: the causal (look-ahead) attention mask.** We mask out all *future* positions in the attention so each token can only attend to itself and earlier tokens.

```
Attention allowed (✓) / blocked (✗):
        tok1  tok2  tok3  tok4
 tok1    ✓     ✗     ✗     ✗
 tok2    ✓     ✓     ✗     ✗
 tok3    ✓     ✓     ✓     ✗
 tok4    ✓     ✓     ✓     ✓
```

This is the **masked self-attention** we mentioned in the decoder on Day 2. It enforces honesty: the model learns to predict the future using only the past — exactly the situation it faces when generating for real.

> Teacher forcing = learn from the correct history (and train in parallel). Causal masking = never peek at the future. Together they make generative training both fast and honest.
slides.speakerNotes: Two tricks, two problems — keep them paired. Teacher forcing = the piano-teacher analogy (placing your fingers on the right keys). Causal mask = the 'no peeking at the answer sheet' rule, and tie it directly back to the masked self-attention triangle from Day 2. Show the triangular mask grid — it's the clearest possible picture. Mention exposure bias briefly so advanced learners feel seen. Ask: 'If we let the model see future words while learning to predict them, what would it learn?' (To cheat by copying — useless at generation time.)
slides.marketConnection: Exposure bias (train on truth, generate on own output) is one root cause of the 'hallucination snowball' — once a 2026 model emits one wrong fact, it conditions on its own mistake. It's a live research and product-trust issue.

slides.title: The Context Window: The Model's Working Memory
slides.body:
## What the model can 'see' at once

You'll hear '128K context,' '1 million token context' constantly in 2026. What does it actually mean?

The **context window** is the maximum number of tokens the model can consider **at one time** — everything you typed *plus* everything it has generated so far. It is the model's **working memory** (like RAM), not its long-term knowledge (which lives baked in the weights from training).

```
[ ──────────── context window (e.g., 128,000 tokens) ──────────── ]
  your system prompt + your question + uploaded docs + chat history + the model's reply-so-far
```

### Crucial truths about context

1. **It's a hard limit.** Exceed it and the oldest tokens fall off the edge — the model literally cannot see them anymore. This is why a very long chat 'forgets' how it started.
2. **The model has no memory between separate conversations** (unless an app stores and re-injects it). Each request re-sends the relevant context. The model itself is stateless.
3. **Bigger isn't free.** Attention cost grows quickly with length (classically quadratic — every token attends to every token), so longer context means more compute, more money, and often slower responses. This is why long context was *the* hard engineering problem of 2023-2026.
4. **'Lost in the middle.'** Research shows models attend best to the start and end of a long context and can overlook information buried in the middle — long context is necessary but not magic.

### Why it matters in practice

The context window is what lets you paste a whole contract, codebase, or book and ask questions about it. It's also the constraint behind **RAG** (retrieval-augmented generation): when your knowledge base is bigger than the window, you *retrieve* only the most relevant chunks and slot them into the context.

> Context window = how much the model can hold in its head right now. Training weights = what it permanently knows. Don't confuse the two.
slides.speakerNotes: The single most important clarification: context window = RAM (temporary, this conversation), training = long-term knowledge (permanent). People constantly confuse them. Drive the consequences: long chats forget the start; the model is stateless between calls; longer context costs more (quadratic) and isn't perfectly used ('lost in the middle'). Connect to RAG as the workaround. Ask: 'If you have a 10,000-page manual but a 128K-token window, how do you let the model answer questions about it?' (Retrieve relevant pages — that's RAG.)
slides.marketConnection: Context window size is a headline spec in every 2026 launch — Gemini and others advertise 1M-2M tokens, and 'context engineering' (what to put in the window and where) is a hot job skill. The quadratic cost is why efficient-attention and memory architectures attract heavy investment.

slides.title: Decoding Part 1: Greedy and Beam Search
slides.body:
## The model gives probabilities — but how do we pick words?

At each step the model outputs a probability over the whole vocabulary (thanks to softmax). But it must emit **one** token. *How* we choose is called **decoding**, and it dramatically changes the output. Today: the two 'find the most likely' strategies.

### Greedy decoding — always grab the top pick

The simplest strategy: at each step, pick the single **highest-probability** token. Fast and deterministic.

```
"The weather today is" → [sunny 0.4, cloudy 0.3, nice 0.2, ...]
greedy picks → "sunny" (always)
```

**The flaw:** greedy is short-sighted. The locally best word can lead to a globally worse sentence — like always taking the nearest exit and missing the highway. It also produces **repetitive, bland** text ('I think that I think that...') and never explores alternatives.

### Beam search — keep several candidate sentences alive

Beam search hedges: instead of committing to one word, it keeps the top **B** partial sequences (the 'beams', e.g., B=5) at every step, expands each, and keeps the best B overall. At the end it returns the highest-probability *whole sequence*.

```
beam width = 3:
step1: keep top 3 first-words
step2: expand each → score whole sequences → keep best 3
...    → finally pick the best complete sentence
```

**Strength:** finds higher-probability overall sequences than greedy — great for tasks with a 'correct' answer like **translation** and **summarization**.

**Weakness:** still tends toward safe, generic, repetitive text, and it's more expensive. For open-ended *creative* generation, the 'most probable' sentence is often the most boring one — which is why chatbots rarely use pure beam search.

> Greedy and beam both chase **probability**. That's perfect when there's a right answer, but deadly for creativity. To get interesting text, we need to deliberately add *randomness* — the dials on the next slide.
slides.speakerNotes: Set up the core tension: the model gives a distribution, but we must choose one word, and HOW we choose changes everything. Greedy = always take the top pick = short-sighted and bland (the 'nearest exit, miss the highway' analogy). Beam = keep several sentences alive = better for translation/summarization where there's a right answer. The key takeaway to seed for the next slide: 'most probable' often = 'most boring.' Ask: 'Why might the single most-likely sentence be the dullest one?' (Because the safest, most generic continuations are the most probable.)
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, beam search still powers backends where there's a correct output (machine translation, speech-to-text, structured extraction), while consumer chatbots use the sampling methods on the next slide to sound natural and varied.

slides.title: Decoding Part 2: Temperature, Top-k, Top-p — The Creativity Dials
slides.body:
## The dials that make a model boring or brilliant

To get natural, varied text, we **sample** from the probability distribution instead of always taking the top word. Three controls shape that sampling — and these are the dials behind 'temperature' settings you see in every 2026 API.

### Temperature — the chaos knob

Temperature reshapes the distribution *before* sampling:
- **Low temperature (e.g., 0.2):** sharpens it — the top tokens dominate. Output is focused, deterministic, safe. Great for facts, code, math.
- **High temperature (e.g., 1.2):** flattens it — unlikely tokens get a real chance. Output is creative, surprising, sometimes incoherent. Great for brainstorming, poetry.
- **Temperature = 0:** effectively greedy (always the top token).

```
logits → divide by temperature → softmax → sample
low temp  = peaky distribution  = predictable
high temp = flat distribution   = adventurous
```

### Top-k sampling — only consider the k best words

Restrict sampling to the **k most likely** tokens (e.g., k=40), then sample among those. This blocks the bizarre long-tail tokens while still allowing variety. *Limitation:* a fixed k is clumsy — sometimes 40 options are all reasonable, sometimes only 2 are.

### Top-p (nucleus) sampling — the smart, adaptive cutoff

Instead of a fixed count, keep the smallest set of top tokens whose probabilities **add up to p** (e.g., p=0.9), then sample from that 'nucleus.'

```
if one word is 95% likely → nucleus is just that 1 word (confident → focused)
if 30 words share the mass → nucleus includes ~30 (uncertain → diverse)
```

Top-p adapts to the model's confidence — wide when many options are plausible, narrow when one is clearly right. This is why **top-p is the most popular default** in modern systems, often combined with a moderate temperature.

### Putting it together

```
Task                 Suggested dials
-------------------  ------------------------------
Factual Q&A, code    temp ~0-0.3 (or greedy)
General chat         temp ~0.7, top-p ~0.9
Creative writing     temp ~1.0+, top-p ~0.95
Translation          beam search (no sampling)
```

> Same model, same weights — but turn these dials and it shifts from a cautious accountant to a wild poet. Decoding is where you, the user, control the personality of the output.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the most immediately practical slide of the day — people will use it tomorrow. Temperature = chaos knob (low=safe/factual, high=creative/risky). Top-k = fixed shortlist; top-p = smart adaptive shortlist that's wide when the model's unsure and narrow when it's confident — explain WHY top-p won as the default. Walk the task->dials table; that's the cheat sheet they'll screenshot. Demo if possible: same prompt at temp 0.2 vs 1.2. Ask: 'For generating legal contract text vs a birthday poem, which way do you turn temperature?' (Down vs up.)
slides.marketConnection: Every 2026 LLM API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) exposes temperature and top-p as parameters; reasoning models often run near-deterministic (low temp) for math/code, while creative-writing products crank it up. 'Tune the temperature' is now everyday vocabulary for AI builders.

slides.title: The Full Lifecycle: Tying It All Together
slides.body:
## From your keystroke to the model's reply — the complete journey

Let's assemble everything from Days 1-3 into one continuous story. You type: **'Write a haiku about the sea.'**

```
1. TOKENIZE   your text → subword tokens (BPE)
               ["Write"," a"," hai","ku"," about"," the"," sea","."]

2. EMBED      each token → meaning vector  + positional encoding

3. CONTEXT    all of it sits in the context window (working memory)

4. FORWARD    flows through stacked Transformer layers (Day 2):
               self-attention (gather) → feed-forward (think), xN
               with causal masking — each token sees only the past

5. PREDICT    final layer → logits over the whole vocabulary
               → softmax → probability for every possible next token

6. DECODE     a strategy picks the next token
               (creative task → temperature ~1.0, top-p ~0.95)

7. APPEND     chosen token is added to the sequence...
               ...and we loop back to step 4 for the NEXT token
               (autoregressive generation — one token at a time)

8. STOP       until an <end> token or length limit → detokenize → text
```

### The two halves of a model's mind

- **What it permanently knows** = the weights, frozen after training (the next-token game, played at planetary scale, with cross-entropy loss and backprop).
- **What it's thinking about right now** = the context window, refreshed every request.

### The full arc of the course so far

- **Day 1:** *Why* the Transformer exists — attention replaced recurrence, unlocking scale.
- **Day 2:** *How* it's built — Q/K/V, multi-head, positional encoding, the stack.
- **Day 3:** *How it learns and speaks* — tokens, embeddings, next-token training, and the decoding dials.

> You can now trace a single sentence from your keyboard, through tokenization and embeddings, across attention layers, into a probability distribution, and back out as generated text — and explain every step. That is real understanding of how modern AI works.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the grand integration of three days — narrate it as one unbroken film from keystroke to reply, calling back to specific slides as you go ('remember Q/K/V from Day 2? that's step 4'). The most important conceptual close: the two halves of the mind — frozen weights (permanent knowledge) vs context window (current thoughts). Emphasize the autoregressive LOOP — steps 4-7 repeat for every single token, which is why responses stream. Ask the room to narrate the lifecycle back to you in their own words; that retrieval cements all three days.
slides.marketConnection: This exact loop runs every time you use any 2026 AI product. Understanding it explains real behaviors users see daily: why responses stream token-by-token, why long chats forget, why the same prompt gives different answers (sampling), and why factual prompts should use low temperature.

slides.title: Recap + Quick Check
slides.body:
## Lock in how models learn to speak

### The day in eight ideas

1. **Tokenization** — text becomes subword pieces (BPE/WordPiece); models see chunks, not letters.
2. **Embeddings** — tokens become meaning-vectors; similarity becomes geometry.
3. **Next-token prediction** — one game, played at planetary scale, teaches grammar, facts, reasoning.
4. **Masked LM** — the bidirectional, fill-in-the-blank variant (BERT-style) for understanding.
5. **Softmax + cross-entropy loss** — turn scores into bets, then punish confident wrongness.
6. **Teacher forcing + causal masks** — train fast (in parallel) and honestly (no peeking ahead).
7. **Context window** — working memory (RAM), distinct from baked-in knowledge (weights).
8. **Decoding** — greedy/beam chase probability; temperature/top-k/top-p add controllable creativity.

---

### Quick check (think-pair-share)

**1.** Why do models use subword tokens instead of whole words or characters? Give one advantage of each over the extremes.

**2.** What does the famous 'king - man + woman ≈ queen' tell us about embeddings?

**3.** Explain how predicting the next token forces a model to learn facts and grammar — without ever being explicitly taught them.

**4.** What's the difference between a model's context window and its training weights?

**5.** You want consistent, factual answers for a calculator app. Which decoding settings do you choose, and why?

**6.** You want a wildly creative brainstorm. Which dials do you turn, and which way?

*(Sketch answers: 1. Subwords = small vocab + no unknown words + efficient; beats huge word vocab and over-long char sequences. 2. Relationships are encoded as consistent directions in vector space. 3. To predict well across all text, it must implicitly model grammar/facts/logic. 4. Context = temporary working memory for this request; weights = permanent learned knowledge. 5. Low temperature / greedy for determinism. 6. High temperature + high top-p for diversity.)*
slides.speakerNotes: Recite the eight-idea summary together, then run the quick check as think-pair-share. The two highest-value checks are Q3 (why next-token prediction yields intelligence — the soul of the day) and Q4 (context vs weights — the most common real-world confusion). Q5 and Q6 are practical and fun — let people argue settings. Close the three-day arc explicitly: 'You now understand WHY the Transformer exists, HOW it's built, and HOW it learns and speaks. You can read the 2026 AI market as an insider.' Then tee up Day 4.
slides.marketConnection: Everything reviewed here is the working vocabulary of 2026 AI practitioners — tokens (pricing/limits), embeddings (search/RAG), context windows (specs), and decoding params (API knobs). Learners can now read product docs and model cards fluently.

keyTakeaways: Models work in subword tokens (BPE/WordPiece), not whole words or letters — which is why they're efficient but can fumble letter-level tasks.
Embeddings turn tokens into meaning-vectors where similarity is distance and relationships are directions (king - man + woman ≈ queen).
A single self-supervised game — predict the next token at planetary scale — forces the model to learn grammar, facts, and reasoning with no human labels.
Softmax turns scores into probabilities; cross-entropy loss punishes confident wrongness; backprop nudges every weight to reduce that loss.
Teacher forcing trains in parallel using the correct history; causal masks stop the model from peeking at future tokens — fast and honest training.
Context window is temporary working memory (distinct from baked-in weights), and decoding dials (temperature, top-k, top-p) control the creativity of every answer.
marketSnapshot2026: Every concept today is a daily reality in the 2026 AI market. You are billed per token, so tokenization economics matter; embedding models are a thriving product category powering semantic search and the retrieval step of RAG, which enterprises everywhere use to 'chat with their documents.' Next-token pretraining remains the foundation of all frontier models, now topped with reasoning-focused reinforcement-learning stages that reward correct step-by-step solutions. Context windows are a headline spec — vendors advertise 1M-2M tokens — while 'context engineering' (deciding what to place in the window, and where, given 'lost in the middle' effects) has become a recognized skill. And the decoding dials are exposed in every major API: low temperature for code and math in reasoning models, higher temperature with top-p for natural conversation and creative writing. A learner who finishes today can read any 2026 model card, pricing page, or launch announcement and understand exactly what is being claimed.
homework: Hands-on: open any AI chat tool or API playground you have access to. (1) Find where you can set temperature (or use one tool for facts and one prompt for creativity). Ask the SAME prompt (e.g., 'Give me a tagline for a coffee shop') three times at a low setting and three times at a high setting; note how variety and quality change. (2) Estimate the token count of a one-page document (rule of thumb: ~0.75 words per token, so words ÷ 0.75 ≈ tokens) and check whether it would fit in a 128K context window. Write half a page on what you observed about the temperature dial and what surprised you.
nextDayTeaser: We've built and trained the Transformer — but raw next-token prediction makes a model that completes text, not one that helpfully answers you. Tomorrow we cross into the modern era: how a raw base model becomes a helpful, harmless assistant through instruction tuning and RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback), the birth of GPT and the ChatGPT moment that changed the world, and why 'alignment' became the most important — and most contested — word in AI. The story is about to get human.
